algorithms.title: Pediatric Assessment Triangle and ABCDE Algorithm
algorithms.description: A structured approach to the initial assessment of an acutely ill or injured child, beginning with the rapid hands-off PAT and progressing to the systematic ABCDE primary assessment.
steps: Step 1 — Observe the child from across the room (hands-off): Assess Appearance (TICLS: Tone, Interactiveness, Consolability, Look/gaze, Speech/cry), Breathing (work of breathing, audible sounds), and Circulation (skin color, mucous membranes).

Step 2 — Categorize severity based on the PAT: All normal = minor problem (monitor and reassess); One abnormal = moderate problem (detailed assessment and treatment); Two or three abnormal = severe / life-threatening (immediate intervention).
Step 3 — If the PAT is abnormal, begin the primary ABCDE assessment. A = Airway: open and assess patency; position the head, suction if needed, consider jaw thrust, foreign body, and adjuncts.
Step 4 — B = Breathing: assess rate, depth, work of breathing, oxygen saturation, and auscultate lung sounds. Provide bag-mask ventilation or advanced airway if breathing is inadequate.

Step 5 — C = Circulation: assess heart rate, pulse quality, capillary refill, skin color/temperature, blood pressure. Establish IV/IO access, administer fluid bolus if indicated (20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid for shock).
Step 6 — D = Disability: assess neurological status (AVPU or GCS), pupil size and reactivity, and check blood glucose. Treat seizures or hypoglycemia as identified.
Step 7 — E = Exposure: fully undress the child to identify rashes, injuries, distension, or other findings while maintaining warmth.
Step 8 — Reassess after each ABCDE step and after each intervention. Children can deteriorate rapidly; continuous reassessment is essential.
Step 9 — Transition to the secondary assessment (SAMPLE history and head-to-toe exam) once the primary assessment is stabilized.
Step 10 — Re-categorize severity using the assess–categorize–decide–act framework and escalate or de-escalate care accordingly.

algorithms.title: Respiratory Category Identification and Initial Management
algorithms.description: Classifies pediatric respiratory distress into three anatomic categories and guides initial management based on the category identified.
steps: Assess the child's work of breathing and auscultate lung sounds.

If stridor and inspiratory distress → Upper Airway Obstruction: consider foreign body (direct laryngoscopy), croup (nebulized epinephrine, dexamethasone), epiglottitis (do NOT agitate; prepare for controlled airway), anaphylaxis (epinephrine IM).

If wheezing and prolonged expiratory phase → Lower Airway Obstruction: consider asthma (bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids), bronchiolitis (supportive care, consider HFNC), pneumonia (antibiotics, respiratory support).

If tachypnea with clear lungs and cyanosis → Pulmonary Vascular Obstruction: consider pulmonary embolism (anticoagulation, consultation) or congenital heart disease with right-to-left shunting (cardiology consultation, prostaglandin E1 if ductal-dependent).
Reassess after each intervention and adjust management based on the child's response.

algorithms.title: Assess–Categorize–Decide–Act Framework
algorithms.description: The PALS four-step clinical decision-making framework applied to any pediatric emergency encounter.
steps: Assess: Perform the PAT and primary ABCDE assessment. Gather clinical data through observation and targeted examination.

Categorize: Classify the child's severity — mild (one system involved, well-compensated), moderate (one or two systems, partially compensated), or severe / life-threatening (multiple systems, decompensated or pre-arrest).
Decide: Choose the treatment priority — what interventions are needed, how urgently, and in what setting (emergency department, ICU, operating room). Identify reversible causes.
Act: Initiate the planned interventions — airway management, ventilation, fluids, medications, or transfer. Then reassess continuously and repeat the framework as the child's condition evolves.
